title: Zaluzhnyi's Verdict: Operational-Level Warfare Is Dead

source.original: General Valerii Zaluzhnyi on the Impossibility of Operational-Level Tasks Due to Technological Progress — Giorgi Revishvili Thread

body_html:
General Valerii Zaluzhnyi — the man who commanded Ukraine's armed forces through the most intense phase of the war against Russia — has made a stark declaration: scientific and technological progress has rendered traditional operational-level military tasks effectively impossible. This is not an academic musing from a think-tank analyst. It comes from a commander who tried to execute exactly these tasks on a modern battlefield and watched the friction firsthand.

Zaluzhnyi's argument centers on the idea that the classical playbook of massing forces for breakthrough operations, exploiting gaps, and achieving operational-level surprise has been shattered by the convergence of pervasive ISR, drones, precision-guided munitions, and networked fires. Every attempt to concentrate combat power becomes visible almost immediately and is met with devastating, responsive fires before it can achieve decisive effect.

This represents a fundamental challenge to the way major militaries have organized, trained, and equipped themselves for decades. The operational level of war — the bridge between tactical engagements and strategic objectives — has been the conceptual heart of Western military doctrine since at least the Cold War. If Zaluzhnyi is right, that entire intellectual edifice needs to be reconsidered.

The implications ripple outward into force design, procurement, and alliance planning. If large-scale maneuver is no longer viable, then the heavy combined-arms formations that NATO has long prioritized may be structurally mismatched to the battlefield they would actually face. The future may belong to distributed, smaller-scale units capable of surviving under persistent surveillance.

What makes Zaluzhnyi's assessment uniquely difficult to dismiss is his dual authority: he has both commanded forces in the largest conventional war in Europe since 1945 and now occupies a diplomatic role where strategic communication matters. His words carry the weight of direct experience and institutional consequence simultaneously.
